commands.repair_reason: - [P2] This protected maintainer draft needs author/owner follow-up for the MCP schema bug, broad compatibility/auth-provider acceptance, overlap coordination, and exact-head proof refresh rather than an automated repla...; Needs attention: The diff is security/availability hardening with no dependency or workflow supply-chain change, but the MCP nested union reader still leaves a hostile plugin-schema crash path.;... (sha=8262c29e0bf968df71e8f48ddd3b3e10bce9bc97)
commands.review_summary:
The branch hardens plugin, schema, provider, Codex, Gateway, and outbound metadata readers with synthetic fuzz coverage so malformed plugin/config/tool data is quarantined, omitted, or denied instead of crashing or widening runtime behavior.

Reproducibility: yes. for the review finding from source inspection: a loopback tool with `parameters.anyOf` containing an object whose `properties` getter throws reaches `flattenUnionSchema` and throws before quarantine. I did not run tests because this review was read-only.
commands.review_followup: - [P2] This protected maintainer draft needs author/owner follow-up for the MCP schema bug, broad compatibility/auth-provider acceptance, overlap coordination, and exact-head proof refresh rather than an automated replacement branch.

target.title: fix(plugin): harden schema and metadata fuzz boundaries
target.body:
## Summary

- Hardened runtime/plugin schema and metadata boundaries so malformed or unreadable synthetic plugin data is quarantined, omitted, diagnosed, audited, or treated as absent instead of crashing assistant startup, doctor/setup flows, provider routing, Gateway catalogs, MCP loopback `tools/list` / `tools/call`, Codex app-server routing, runtime schema projection, system-prompt/report generation, prompt-cache observability, or outbound action handling.
- Kept tests synthetic: current fixtures use names like `fuzzplugin`, `mockplugin`, and `fuzz_move_*`, not the reported third-party plugin name.
- Latest slice guards Gateway MCP loopback tool schema and call lookup so one unreadable tool name or parameter schema cannot crash catalog listing, poison healthy sibling calls, or execute a tool that was not advertised.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

No linked closing issue.

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

No linked related issue.

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

Maintainer-directed hardening/debugging work.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: malformed runtime plugin tool schemas and hostile plugin/config/provider/manifest/native-approval/outbound-action metadata can crash, widen decisions, or bypass schema quarantine before normal OpenClaw diagnostics, setup, provider routing, gateway startup planning, Gateway catalog rendering, MCP loopback tool catalog/call handling, approval forwarding, manifest/capability auth gating, local-model lean filtering, realtime voice consult tool selection, MCP plugin-tool serving, message-provider policy filtering, embedded-agent tool-name allowlist collection, deferred follow-up tool description rewriting, Tool Search / Code Mode cataloging, Codex dynamic-tool filtering, message action validation, tool policy filtering/audit, provider tool-schema compatibility traversal, embedded-agent schema diagnostics, plugin runtime tool resolution, central runtime schema projection, embedded system-prompt rendering, system-prompt report construction, prompt-cache observability, Codex model-call diagnostic payload construction, Codex prompt-context report assembly, Codex developer-instruction generation, Codex workspace memory tool routing, agent tool-definition projection, or Codex app-server handling complete.

Real environment tested: local direct source probes, local focused lint/format checks, local focused Vitest through `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs`, and earlier broad remote validation on direct AWS Crabbox Linux runners. Blacksmith Testbox was unavailable in this shell because `blacksmith auth login` is required.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

- Rebased cleanly onto `origin/main` `6c309b9883a74c1ba2383321872aa46b3d2d6f16`.
- Direct red source probe for Gateway MCP loopback schema construction with a synthetic `fuzzplugin` tool whose `name` getter throws; pre-fix result was the expected raw throw.
- Direct green source assertion for `buildMcpToolSchema` covering unreadable synthetic tool names, unreadable parameter schemas, and healthy `mockplugin_lookup` sibling preservation.
- `CI=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-core.config.ts src/gateway/mcp-http.test.ts -t "(omits unreadable loopback tool names|calls healthy tools when an earlier loopback tool name is unreadable|does not execute invalid-name loopback tools|does not execute loopback tools whose parameter schema is unreadable)" --reporter=verbose`
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check src/gateway/mcp-http.schema.ts src/gateway/mcp-http.handlers.ts src/gateway/mcp-http.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/gateway/mcp-http.schema.ts src/gateway/mcp-http.handlers.ts src/gateway/mcp-http.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- Current branch diff scrub for the reported plugin name.
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`
- Earlier latest slices also passed direct red/green source assertions plus oxfmt, targeted oxlint, `git diff --check`, scrub checks, and clean local autoreview for agent tool-definition projection, Codex developer instructions, Codex prompt-context reports, Codex model-call diagnostics, and prompt-cache observability.
- Earlier branch proofs include focused Vitest for existing slices and AWS Crabbox `pnpm check:changed` gates, including provider `aws`, lease `cbx_991a8c29acdf`, run `run_41811346ac17`, exit 0; and provider `aws`, lease `cbx_52b1fe398bd3`, run `run_c85914033aaa`, exit 0.

Evidence after fix:

- Latest branch head pushed: `8262c29e0bf968df71e8f48ddd3b3e10bce9bc97`.
- Latest PR base: `origin/main` `6c309b9883a74c1ba2383321872aa46b3d2d6f16`.
- Gateway MCP loopback `tools/list` now skips tools with unreadable or blank names, treats unreadable descriptions as absent, and omits tools whose parameter schema cannot be read.
- Gateway MCP loopback `tools/call` now rejects blank or non-string requested names, only executes names present in the advertised tool schema, and uses the same bounded name reader for executable lookup.
- Agent tool-definition projection now skips unreadable tool names, defaults unreadable optional fields, and preserves healthy sibling tools.
- Codex developer-instruction generation now skips unreadable or blank deferred dynamic tool names, treats unreadable `deferLoading` as not deferred, and still preserves healthy visible-reply and deferred-tool guidance.
- Codex prompt-context report construction now skips unreadable or blank tool names, treats unreadable descriptions as empty, reads `deferLoading` and `inputSchema` through bounded access, and preserves healthy sibling report entries.
- Codex workspace memory routing, Codex model-call diagnostics, and prompt-cache observability now skip unreadable or blank tool names and preserve healthy sibling definitions/names.
- Current branch diff, PR body, and spec scrubs pass for the reported plugin name.
- Local autoreview was clean with no accepted/actionable findings after the latest Gateway loopback slice.

Observed result after fix:

- Hostile synthetic config/schema/provider/manifest/native-approval/message-action/plugin-tool inputs are bounded at the relevant owner boundary instead of throwing raw getter/enumeration/serialization/spread errors.
- Gateway MCP loopback can list and call healthy `mockplugin` tools even when a sibling synthetic `fuzzplugin` tool exposes an unreadable `name` getter.
- Gateway MCP loopback does not advertise or execute a tool whose parameter schema cannot be read, and malformed `tools/call` requests do not match invalid hidden tools.
- Existing focused slices continue to preserve healthy siblings while omitting only malformed descriptors across local-model lean filtering, embedded-agent allowlists, message-provider filtering, deferred follow-up descriptions, realtime voice consult tools, MCP plugin-tool serving, embedded system prompts, central schema projection, provider tools, system-prompt reports, prompt-cache diagnostics, Gateway catalogs, Codex report/instruction paths, and outbound message actions.

What was not tested:

- Blacksmith Testbox `pnpm check:changed` was not run because `blacksmith` is not authenticated in this shell.
- The latest broad Crabbox gates predate the newest prompt-cache, Codex diagnostics, Codex prompt-context, Codex developer-instruction, agent tool-definition adapter, and Gateway MCP loopback slices; these slices have focused local proof, static proof, scrub proof, and clean local review where non-mechanical code changed.
- Full `src/agents/code-mode.test.ts` remains locally blocked by the linked worktree `quickjs-wasi` export issue noted in prior PR updates.
- GitHub PR checks are queued/running on the pushed head.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No new config surface. Existing malformed/unreadable config, manifest, provider, tool descriptor, diagnostic, catalog, dynamic-tool, or action inputs now fail closed or resolve as absent at bounded reads.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Malformed or unreadable tool/provider/plugin/native-approval/action metadata is rejected, omitted, or quarantined earlier instead of crashing runtime paths, bypassing schema quarantine, broadening access, or executing an unadvertised MCP loopback tool.

What is the highest-risk area?

Overly strict sanitation could omit a valid provider tool descriptor, plugin tool descriptor, Tool Search catalog entry, Gateway catalog entry, MCP loopback tool, Codex dynamic tool, MCP plugin-tool descriptor, realtime custom voice tool, diagnostic payload, runtime projection descriptor, prompt-only tool name, prompt-cache diagnostic tool name, Codex model-call diagnostic tool definition, Codex prompt-context report entry, Codex developer-instruction deferred tool entry, Codex workspace memory tool route, message-provider-visible tool, deferred-followup-visible tool, embedded-agent allowlist-visible tool, agent tool-definition entry, or outbound action parameter.

How is that risk mitigated?

The changes preserve readable sibling entries, default only parameterless client catalog schemas or unreadable local tool-definition parameters to `{}`, fail closed only for explicit route/control/identity/idempotency-style surfaces, keep unreadable payload-only fields as absent, require MCP loopback calls to match advertised schema names, preserve duplicate readable tool counts in message-provider filtering, keep deferred follow-up unreadable names as non-matching opaque entries, and use focused regressions plus earlier broad AWS `check:changed` gates.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Review the draft PR at head `e9bef99e70e77e56348d66fc438e5f3d7765e7a8`, or continue the next fuzzing slice from the current branch.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

GitHub CI is queued/running after the force-push. Blacksmith Testbox proof is blocked on local authentication. No local actionable autoreview findings remain.
target.author: vincentkoc
